questions.question:
Let P_1, P_2, ..., P_k be points in the xy-plane such that no three of them are collinear. For every pair of points P_i and P_j, let L_{ij} be the line passing through them. Let L_{ab} be the line with the steepest gradient amongst all lines L_{ij}.

Which one of the following properties should necessarily be satisfied?

A. P_a and P_b are adjacent to each other with respect to their x-coordinates.
B. Either P_a or P_b has the largest or smallest y-coordinate amongst all the points.
C. The difference between the y-coordinates of P_a and P_b is minimum.
D. None of the above.
questions.answer: A. P_a and P_b are adjacent to each other with respect to their x-coordinates.
questions.to_find: Property that the two points defining the steepest line must necessarily satisfy

understand.plain: The slope of a line through two points is (y_2 - y_1)/(x_2 - x_1). For the slope to be steepest (maximum), we want the y-difference to be large relative to the x-difference. If two points are not adjacent in x-order, there is always another pair with a smaller x-gap but similar y-gap, giving a larger slope. So the steepest line must connect x-adjacent points.

keywords.explain: Rise over run: (y_b - y_a)/(x_b - x_a). Steeper means larger absolute value.
keywords.example: Points (1,0) and (2,5) give slope 5; points (1,0) and (3,5) give slope 2.5.

keywords.explain: Two points are x-adjacent if no other point has an x-coordinate strictly between them.
keywords.example: Among x-values {1, 3, 5}, the pairs (1,3) and (3,5) are adjacent; (1,5) is not.

keywords.explain: Three points are collinear if they all lie on one straight line. The problem forbids this, ensuring unique line slopes.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 420 260' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='420' height='260' rx='10' class='bg'/><line x1='40' y1='220' x2='380' y2='220' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='40' y1='220' x2='40' y2='30' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='390' y='225' class='sub mut'>x</text><text x='28' y='28' class='sub mut'>y</text><circle cx='80' cy='180' r='6' class='ok'/><circle cx='160' cy='140' r='6' class='ok'/><circle cx='240' cy='60' r='8' class='hi' style='stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)'/><circle cx='300' cy='100' r='8' class='hi' style='stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)'/><circle cx='360' cy='150' r='6' class='ok'/><line x1='240' y1='60' x2='300' y2='100' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='85' y='175' class='sub ink'>P1</text><text x='165' y='135' class='sub ink'>P2</text><text x='218' y='52' class='sub ink'>Pa</text><text x='305' y='95' class='sub ink'>Pb</text><text x='362' y='148' class='sub ink'>P5</text><text x='250' y='85' class='tag' style='fill:#7F77DD'>steepest</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Five points on the xy-plane where the two x-adjacent points Pa and Pb (the purple pair) form the line with the steepest slope among all pairs.

given.aim: Identify a necessary property of the two points that define the line with steepest gradient.

terms.meaning: k points in the plane, no three collinear.
terms.connects: Every pair defines a line L_{ij} with a well-defined unique slope.

terms.meaning: The line through P_a and P_b that has the maximum (steepest) slope among all pairs.
terms.example: If slopes are 1, 3, 5, 4, then L_{ab} is the pair giving slope 5.
terms.connects: We want to characterize P_a and P_b.

terms.meaning: P_a and P_b are x-adjacent if no other P_i has an x-coordinate strictly between x_a and x_b.
terms.example: Sort points by x; consecutive points in this ordering are x-adjacent.
terms.connects: Option A claims this must hold for the steepest pair.

given.plan: Assume for contradiction that P_a and P_b are NOT x-adjacent. Then there exists P_c with x_a < x_c < x_b. The slope of L_{ab} equals (y_b - y_a)/(x_b - x_a). Using the intermediate point, we can show that at least one of the slopes (L_{ac} or L_{cb}) is at least as steep, contradicting the maximality of L_{ab}. Hence P_a and P_b must be x-adjacent.

steps.title: Set up slope formula and assume non-adjacency
steps.apply: Suppose P_a and P_b are not x-adjacent. Then there exists P_c with x_a < x_c < x_b. Let s = (y_b - y_a)/(x_b - x_a) be the slope of L_{ab}.
steps.note: WLOG assume x_a < x_b (relabel if needed).

steps.title: Show x-adjacent pair has slope >= s
steps.formula_raw: s = (y_b - y_a)/(x_b - x_a) = weighted average of s_{ac} and s_{cb}
steps.apply: The slope s is a weighted average: s = [(x_c - x_a)*s_{ac} + (x_b - x_c)*s_{cb}] / (x_b - x_a). So max(s_{ac}, s_{cb}) >= s. This means L_{ab} is not the steepest line, a contradiction.
steps.note: A weighted average is at most the maximum of its parts, so at least one of s_{ac}, s_{cb} meets or exceeds s.

steps.title: Conclude option A is necessary
steps.apply: By contradiction, P_a and P_b must be adjacent with respect to their x-coordinates. Options B, C, D can be ruled out by simple counterexamples.
steps.note: Option A is the only necessary property.

solution.result: A. P_a and P_b are adjacent to each other with respect to their x-coordinates.

questions.formula_note: The key insight is that slope is a weighted average of sub-slopes: if two points are not x-adjacent, the intermediate point creates sub-pairs with slope at least as large, so the maximum slope is always achieved by an x-adjacent pair.

questions.question:
The determinant of the matrix

[ 6  -8  1  1 ]
[ 0   2  4  6 ]
[ 0   0  4  8 ]
[ 0   0  0 -1 ]

A. 11    B. -48    C. 0    D. -24
questions.to_find: Determinant of the given 4x4 upper-triangular matrix

understand.plain: For any triangular matrix (upper or lower), the determinant is simply the product of its diagonal entries. This is because row-expansion repeatedly hits zeros off the diagonal, leaving only the diagonal product.

keywords.explain: A square matrix where all entries below the main diagonal are zero.
The determinant equals the product of the diagonal entries.

understand.visual_alt: A 4x4 upper triangular matrix with diagonal entries 6, 2, 4, -1 — all entries below the diagonal are zero.

given.aim: Compute the determinant of the 4x4 upper-triangular matrix.

terms.meaning: The entries a_{11}=6, a_{22}=2, a_{33}=4, a_{44}=-1 lie on the main diagonal.
terms.connects: For a triangular matrix, only diagonal entries contribute to the determinant.

terms.meaning: All entries a_{ij} = 0 for i > j (below the diagonal).
terms.example: The matrix has zeros in rows 2-4 below the diagonal.
terms.connects: This structure guarantees det = product of diagonal entries.

terms.meaning: Expanding along the first column, only the (1,1) entry is nonzero in column 1, reducing to a 3x3 problem.
terms.example: Expanding column 1: only 6 is nonzero, giving 6 * det(lower-right 3x3).
terms.connects: Repeated application gives the diagonal product.

given.plan: Apply the triangular matrix determinant rule: multiply all diagonal entries together.

steps.title: Identify the matrix type
steps.apply: The given matrix is upper triangular (zeros below the diagonal). The diagonal entries are 6, 2, 4, -1.
steps.note: All three zeros in each lower-triangular position confirm this.

steps.title: Compute the product of diagonal entries
steps.note: The sign is negative because of the -1 on the diagonal.

solution.result: det = -48, so the answer is B. -48

questions.formula_note: For a triangular matrix, the determinant equals the product of its diagonal entries — cofactor expansion along any row or column with zeros reduces the problem step by step to this product.

questions.question:
The determinant of the matrix

[ 2  0  0  0 ]
[ 8  1  7  2 ]
[ 2  0  2  0 ]
[ 9  0  6  1 ]

A. 4    B. 0    C. 15    D. 20

understand.plain: When a matrix has many zeros in a column, expanding along that column greatly simplifies the computation. Here, column 2 has three zeros, so only one term survives in the cofactor expansion along column 2, converting a 4x4 determinant into a 3x3 problem.

keywords.explain: Expanding the determinant along a row or column by multiplying each entry by its cofactor (signed minor).
keywords.example: Expanding along a column with three zeros: only one 3x3 minor needs to be evaluated.

keywords.explain: The determinant of the submatrix obtained by deleting row i and column j.
keywords.example: Minor M_{12} = det of matrix with row 1 and column 2 removed.

keywords.explain: Always expand along the row or column with the most zeros to minimize computation.
keywords.example: Column 2 here has entries 0,1,0,0 — only the entry 1 in row 2 contributes.

understand.visual_alt: A 4x4 matrix where column 2 has three zeros, making cofactor expansion along column 2 very efficient.

given.aim: Compute the determinant by expanding along the column with the most zeros.

terms.connects: Column 2 is [0,1,0,0] — ideal for cofactor expansion.

terms.meaning: The cofactor at position (2,2): sign (-1)^(2+2) * det(3x3 minor after removing row 2, col 2).
terms.connects: This is the only surviving term in the column-2 expansion.

terms.meaning: The submatrix [[2,0,0],[2,2,0],[9,6,1]] is lower triangular with diagonal 2,2,1.
terms.connects: Lower triangular determinant = product of diagonal.

given.plan: Expand along column 2 (only one nonzero entry). Compute the resulting 3x3 minor, which is lower triangular.

steps.title: Compute the 3x3 minor M_{22}
steps.apply: Remove row 2 and column 2 from the original matrix: rows 1,3,4 and columns 1,3,4 give [[2,0,0],[2,2,0],[9,6,1]]. This is lower triangular with diagonal entries 2, 2, 1.
steps.note: Lower triangular: all entries above the diagonal are zero.

solution.result: det = 4, so the answer is A. 4

questions.formula_note: The key technique is cofactor expansion along the column with the most zeros — here column 2 has only one nonzero entry, reducing the 4x4 determinant to a single 3x3 lower-triangular minor whose value is immediate.

questions.to_find: Which expression does NOT equal the Vandermonde determinant

understand.plain: The original determinant is a Vandermonde determinant. Options B, C, D are obtained by valid elementary row/column operations that preserve the determinant value. Option A involves swapping columns 2 and 3, which changes the sign, so its determinant is the negative of the original — making it NOT equal to the original.

keywords.explain: det[[1,x,x^2],[1,y,y^2],[1,z,z^2]] = (y-x)(z-x)(z-y), a standard result for 3x3 Vandermonde matrices.
Adding a multiple of one row to another does not change the determinant. Swapping two rows (or columns) changes the sign.
keywords.example: R2 <- R2 - R1 preserves det; swapping C2 and C3 negates det.

keywords.explain: Interchanging two columns multiplies the determinant by -1. Two swaps bring it back to the original sign.
keywords.example: If det(A) = 5, swapping columns 2 and 3 gives det = -5.

understand.visual_alt: The original 3x3 Vandermonde matrix and four candidate matrices; option A has columns 2 and 3 swapped (with x factored out), giving opposite sign.

given.aim: Identify which option does NOT equal the given Vandermonde determinant.

terms.connects: All valid-operation matrices must also give V.

terms.example: Column 2 = x(x+1) = x^2+x, column 3 = x+1. Compare: original has col2 = x, col3 = x^2.
terms.connects: Columns 2 and 3 are swapped relative to the original (after factoring x from col 2). Column swap => det changes sign.

terms.meaning: These use valid row operations: adding a constant to the column (B: adds 1 to col2 and col3 using identity col), subtracting rows (C), or adding adjacent rows (D).
terms.example: C is R2 <- R2 - R1, R3 <- R3 - R1, which preserves the determinant.
terms.connects: All preserve the sign and magnitude of V.

given.plan: Check each option by identifying which elementary operation was applied. Option A involves swapping columns 2 and 3 (with possible factoring), which negates the determinant.

steps.title: Check option C (row subtraction)
steps.formula_raw: R2 <- R2 - R1, R3 <- R3 - R1 preserve det
steps.apply: In option C: row 2 becomes [0, y-x, y^2-x^2] and row 3 becomes [0, z-x, z^2-x^2]. This is R2 - R1 and R3 - R1, which preserves the determinant. So C equals V.
steps.note: Row subtraction is an elementary operation that doesn't change the determinant.

steps.title: Check option D (row addition)
steps.formula_raw: R1_new = R1 + R2, R2_new = R2 + R3, R3_new = R3 + R1 — check sign carefully
steps.apply: Option D has rows [2, x+y, x^2+y^2], [2, y+z, y^2+z^2], [2, z+x, z^2+x^2]. These are pairwise sums of rows of the original: each new row i = row_i + row_j. Adding a multiple of one row to another preserves det, and here it can be verified by cyclic row addition keeping det unchanged. So D equals V.
steps.note: Row-sum operations in a cyclic combination preserve the determinant.

steps.title: Check option A (column swap analysis)
steps.apply: Option A: column 2 = [x(x+1), y(y+1), z(z+1)] = [x^2+x, y^2+y, z^2+z] and column 3 = [x+1, y+1, z+1]. This is C2' = C3_original + C2_original and C3' = C2_original + 1. Comparing with original (C2=x, C3=x^2): the columns have been rearranged so that x^2 terms appear in column 2 and x terms in column 3 — effectively a column swap (plus adding column 1 to each), which gives -V, not V.
steps.note: Swapping any two columns negates the determinant. Hence option A does NOT equal the original determinant.

solution.result: Option A does NOT equal the original Vandermonde determinant.

questions.formula_note: Elementary row/column operations: adding a multiple of one row/column to another preserves the determinant, while swapping two rows/columns negates it. Option A effectively swaps columns 2 and 3 (modulo column-1 additions), giving -V instead of V.
